reviews.review_text:
This is what happens when a developer is passionate, has the time to make the game they want, and they listen to the player's likes, wants, and dislikes and then executes on that feedback.

Recommend playing the first one then the second one in order to get the entire saga.

10/10.  Warhorse mic drop.

"I thought I was kneading dough.  I didn't know she was leaning over the trough i swear!"

Its rare these days to come across a game thats so meticulously detailed, where you can tell from every little corner that the developers were absolutely on fire making this game.

The world feels truly alive, the side quests ive done so far are of a higher standard than the main quests in many other games. For example, i spent two hours searching for my dog in the woods, just only to bump into a drunken thug along the way, and found it thoroughly entertaining.

Henry is a handsome and likeable character and the other characters are all very well done too. NPCs going about their daily business all over the world react to you, have conversations amongst themselves, and sometimes its interesting to watch what some random NPC gets up to during the day.

I really cant find much to criticise about KCD2. What i personally didnt like at all was the combat system. It was fine against one or two opponents, but with more than that i had quite a bit of trouble. Of course, that might just be down to my own lack of skill.
For the most part, I just followed the main quest and now, having finished that, i want to work my way through the side quests.

In any case, i give the game a clear 10/10 Sir Hanses as it does almost nothing wrong but gets an awful lot of things absolutely right.

It took me a while to come around to KCD2 because I usually prefer a bit more fiction and fantasy in my open world RPGs, but there's a great world to explore here that you can really get lost in for a while.

Got myself hooked on blacksmithing early game, scrounging for materials I could hammer into something useful to sell, then whatever I had left after paying for my essentials I gambled away.  Higher stakes gambling led me to a life of crime, building a second set of gear so I could better sneak around at night.

I'd been sticking to the non-combat stuff because after a few very bad combat experiences I thought I must have been undergeared, after training up my skills and getting some adequate gear and a bit of sparring in I tried again, but still ran into major difficulties.  Later found there's a target lock-on system and a controller has a much easier time with the quick directional inputs needed for the ultimate combat technique, an ability called master strike, basically a perfectly timed directional parry that counters an enemy for big damage.

I felt a bit more confident in combat for a time, until unnamed forest bandits started using master strikes against me.  From then on I stuck mostly to melee grapples and master strikes, there's a whole system of combo attacks for all the different weapons you can find, but the problem with any enemy able to pick up a sword being capable of hard countering any attack you can do and often one-shotting you leads you to feeling forced into playing a defensive game of master strikes, just so the enemy doesn't master strike you! After a bit of investigation it seemed a lot of people felt the same way, and modding out master strikes looks very popular.

That was the biggest downside I found to the game, the world is gorgeous and interesting to explore, systems of wear and dirt and blood have you cleaning up your appearance to boost your charisma before talking to others, or you might want to keep some dirt on if you're going thieving at night, there's even a talent to make you stealthier when you're dirtier.

Oh I should mention the save system, I absolutely appreciate the intention here to prevent savescumming by tying saving to a consumable item, resting or exiting the game, however in practice it means a cautious player like myself ends up spending a lot of time harvesting herbs and crafting save game consumables to the point that it feels a bit too grindy for my taste.  Fortunately one of the most popular mods available adds a quicksave, while this does go against the intention of the developers, losing hours of gameplay because of stupid human mistakes felt a bit too punitive for me.  Whether the solution is timed autosaves, regional autosaves or something else I don't know, but as somebody with twitchy mouse button fingers from an injury earlier in life where I can accidentally click the wrong dialogue option more than I'd like, having that quicksave mod was a lifesaver for me.

The ownership system of stolen goods impressed me, where an item description would mention which town you stole an item in, so you would know where NOT to wear that stolen hat.  The whole thievery system felt like a big step up from the elder scrolls system we're all used to, and I'm glad to see that evolution.

Audio is excellent allround, it was cool to hear birdcalls I'd never heard before as I explored forests, the soundtrack was memorable and the voice work ranged from good to excellent.  One minor complaint would be that the pool of voice actors could have been a bit larger, you will hear the same voice actors a lot, but hey that's still infinitely preferrable to AI voice acting.

I'm not usually  one to get super excited by historical settings in my games as I prefer a bit of fiction and fantasy, but the lands of Bohemia grew on me over time, and I'm definitely interested in a possible third Kingdom Come game.

I suppose to summarise, KCD2 stepped into the open world RPG void left by Bethesda and in many ways showed them how to do a better job of it.  I got this on sale for half price and that's a great deal for the experience I got.

Recommended.

How do I begin?

KCD2 is a very refreshing AAA game for the longest time in the midst of AI, remaster, and gameplay replayability. It has been nominated and won several awards in 2025, and now I can see it why. Like most people, I'm one of those who feel playing game nowadays seems like a chore or doesn't feel fun anymore because as a "GaMer" (*hur-dur sarcastically*), I always think how I want to win it ASAP or if I already have the prerequisite knowledge of similar games. Initially, I went blind into KCD2 while avoiding spoilers because I already played KCD in the past....and I'm glad because I feel something that I haven't felt for a long time...I'm actually having fun in single player game. I actually DESIRED it to play more, and that's not an easy exaggeration.

KCD2 is an open-world first-person RPG medieval game where you play as Henry, a friend and squire of Sir Hans Capon. The game sets heavily in historical timeline of Late Middle Ages of 1403 in Bohemia with succession war looming across the Holy Roman Empire. Your role might seem insignificant at first, but you actually play parts in the bigger picture.

Positives:
+ Very good optimization: Warhorse Studio optimization has come a long way since first KCD. I gotta say, at launch, first KCD was not the most optimized version. Even after I played it again somewhere this year, it's still not as optimized as KCD2. But KCD2 actually surprised me with 100-150 fps in Ultra 2K with FSR. There were stutters if you went into some heavily guarded areas if you are running too fast, but it's quite mild. With my PC specs, I never went under 100 fps, so the result might be varied.

+ Great story-writing and easter eggs: you will laugh, you will feel joy, you will question "what if?", you will feel sadness/empathy, but the outcome will be decided by...some dice roll. Yes, that's true. some conversation actually strike by dice rolls similar to DnD. Not too many which feels random, but it's there. Dialogue mechanic options aside, the writing in this game is...very good. Again, it's an RPG game, therefore there are some limitation with to what-if dialogues by the dev, but they basically give Henry options to be an absolute menace or good boi. Sometimes trollish even. And for modern audience, the dev understood perfectly when they put hilarious meme references you saw in internet. Very cultured indeed.

+ Gameplay mechanic and contents: since you could say KCD2 is a game which is quite close to medieval realism, it doesn't need to be as close as historical as possible. But they did. Which feels more fun and less of a medieval history simulator. That doesn't mean the content is not authentic, it's just presented as simplified history version. What I mean is, you must remember this is a GAME and not historical simulator, it doesn't have to be perfect, but at least tolerable to some degree of realism. With that put aside, the content in the game were beautifully designed than I expected and it's hard to mention it without spoiling some story or content.

You want to save your game? Too bad you need to brew a Schnapp for it. You want to buy something? Too bad you are branded now because of your past misdeed. Did you get food poisoning? Better have antidote or you will die in the next 5 minutes (accurate irl too). Ohh, you better check that grave, might be have some quest item for later. Sometimes some quest puzzle like a riddle need an ACTUAL BRAIN to solve it out. Yes, it was that detail. And I love how Warhorse gave insightful Codex to player who are interested in this periodical of time. It's like you are learning and playing at the same time (watching Ubisoft games with face palm). Like depicting NPC's daily life in medieval time of what not and what does. From the architecture of Trotsky castle to Kuttenberg city and (arguably) accurate armor and clothes in the 15th century. There will be lot of historians who will debate this, but still, kudos to Warhorse studios team for putting tremendous effort in the game for the sake of authenticity. 

In short, yes there're lot of contents in the game and justify the price tag.

+ - Good music and sound effect: I don't have lot to say. The music in the game fits perfectly on every action sequence. For example, if you are on the road, you will hear the standard lute and pandeiro so Henry won't be so lonely. Or if you are in Italian quarter, you will hear funny Italian pizza medieval music. For most part, the game will give you sequence of music based on the cinematic, location, and your action in the game. Overall, very good choice of music. That said, the SFX might need some variations. Because most of the SFX sound so high or doesn't have lot of grumble or "thump" variation except the horse hoof gallop. It's not bad per se and certainly won't affect your gameplay, but it's there.

Negatives:
- Minor bugs and soft lock: unfortunately even though I have been such a fanboy about this game, it's with heavy heart that I also must come clean that this game is not without bugs. No game is perfect, and KDC2 is one of those. For example, Kuttenberg Tournament quest notification have such infamous bug where you need to return the gears but it won't allow you, therefore you could just leave the tournament or pay the gear to remove the notification. It's a minor bug I say, but it's annoying because it's such a wake up call. Another example is an investigation quest in Forge DLC where you need to investigate lost materials or schematic for the guild. If you "accidentally" learned one of the required schematic, it will count as lost item and you couldn't lie nor decline the quest to fail it. So the notification dangling there awkwardly and you can't finish the quest. Or maybe you met with strangers in Kuttenberg street, but you couldn't see who you are talking to because they already gone behind the cart in marketplace. For completionist, you better make some Schnapp in case you soft lock some choices (or maybe that's part of the gameplay?).

- Limited save slot: this is just me being very picky. Like aforementioned above, I need to save a lot because I want to try to finish all the side quest first, then the main quest. Therefore I need to save a lot. Turn out, you only could have 500 slots or you need to overwrite the older saves.

- Questionable decent-ish stealth mechanic: this is more like a complaint. Since it's very weird that sometimes enemy could have x-ray vision to see the body inside the building or night vision which could see a body from far away even though it's so damn dark unless you need to get close to it. It's not bad, but it's not good either.

And that's probably it. Kudos to TomMckay (Henry VA), rest of the casts, professional sources, and the developers, you all did brilliantly. Go give KCD2 a try. It's worth the full price, and even better on discount.

The first time I played the original Kingdom Come game way back when it launched. I love the world, simulation but the janky combat very quickly ended my run. Then I came back years later and last month I was able to finish it. I enjoyed it but equally had a frustrating time with it.  With KCD 2, things are different. Sure the combat still carries the jank and there are situations the game throws at you which is frustrating, but only this time the good parts massively outweighted the bad parts.

The combat is tweaked to be faster, a bit simpler. Against one on one, it works really well, but when the game throws you 3 or more enemies, it can be frustrating super fast. Luckily enemies sort of take their turns in attacking  you, unlike KCD 1. 

One design decision they carried over which I am not a fan of at all is their save system. I lost almost 2 hours of gameplay in KCD 2 because of it. I am absolutely not a fan of their quite to save loading, sleep or drink specific item to save. This is an open world simulation driven game, so if the stars alaign, death can come hard and fast. I immediately installed the F5 save mod, cause with it I would not have continued.

The game's story, characters are signficaintly better than the first game. This is where game truly shines above the original. I cared about the characters and the plot a lot more than I did in the first game. Well written with great cast of supporting characters.

The open world also took a big step up. The regions feels much more handcrafted with more attention to detail and diversity than the first game. Every where feels unique, which the first game didd not have. Also the game's biggest town, Kuttenberg is one of the most impressive medieval cities I've ever seen, bustly with life with npc's having their own schedules. All these running on a very impressive performance thanks to their modified Cryengine. The world looks stunning, only the character models and their facial expressions looks dated.

Where the first KCD felt like a 6-7/10 game depending on the day. This game is easily 8-9/10 game. Really enjoyed it. Warhorse Studios, please go away from this restrictive save system, thank you.

LEGENDARY RETURN OF THE KINGDOM 2
Here is a review that can go well on Steam — lively, without dryness, with emotions and a normal balance of pros and cons:
280 hours in Kingdom Come: Deliverance 2. And this is one of those games after which it is difficult to play something “normal” again. I still don’t understand how this game didn’t receive the Game of the Year award.

I played the first part, and I remember how Warhorse did something unique back then: without dragons, magic and superheroes — just a dirty, cruel, living Middle Ages. But the second part is literally everything they wanted to do back then, only now without most of the technical limitations.

The world has become much more alive. Cities look really populated, NPCs no longer seem like decorations, and Indro himself no longer looks like a guy who was accidentally dragged into war — you can feel his growth as a character. Cutscenes, production, dialogues — sometimes I caught myself thinking that this is the level of top RPGs from big studios.
What I liked the most:
— incredible medieval atmosphere
— very strong plot and characters
— music and sound are simply gorgeous
— quests can often be completed in different ways
— the combat system has become more comfortable, but has not lost its “weight”
— the game really drags you along with exploring the world
And most importantly — this game respects the player. It does not lead you by the hand every 5 seconds.

But there are also disadvantages:
— strange optimization in places
— sometimes NPCs behave crookedly
— some animations are still wooden
— some mechanics can suffocate new players in the first hours
However, honestly — all these disadvantages are lost against the background of how great and soulful RPG it is. In 280 hours, I never felt sorry for the time spent. On the contrary — I wanted more.
Kingdom Come: Deliverance 2 is a rare case where a sequel is not just better than the original, but actually builds on all of its ideas. If you liked the first part, you'll feel right at home here. If you haven't played it, it's one of the best RPGs of recent years. This game is completely worth your time and money.

ЛЕГЕНДАРНЕ ПОВЕРНЕННЯ КОРОЛІВСТВА 2
Ось варіант відгуку який може добре зайти в Steam — живий, без сухоти, з емоціями і нормальним балансом плюсів та мінусів:
280 годин у Kingdom Come: Deliverance 2. І це одна з тих ігор, після яких важко знову грати в щось “звичайне”. Досі не розумію як ця гра не отримала премію гри року.

Я проходив ще першу частину, і пам’ятаю як тоді Warhorse зробили щось унікальне: без драконів, магії та супергероїв — просто брудне, жорстоке, живе середньовіччя. Але друга частина — це буквально все те, що вони хотіли зробити ще тоді, тільки тепер без більшості технічних обмежень.
Світ став набагато живішим. Міста виглядають реально населеними, NPC більше не здаються декораціями, а сам Індро вже не виглядає хлопцем якого випадково втягнули у війну — відчувається його ріст як персонажа. Катсцени, постановка, діалоги — іноді ловив себе на думці що це рівень топових RPG від великих студій.

Що найбільше сподобалось:
— неймовірна атмосфера середньовіччя
— дуже сильний сюжет і персонажі
— музика та звук просто шикарні
— квести часто можна проходити різними способами
— бойова система стала комфортнішою, але не втратила свою “вагу”
— гра реально затягує дослідженням світу
І головне — ця гра поважає гравця. Вона не водить тебе за руку кожні 5 секунд.
Але мінуси теж є:
— місцями дивна оптимізація
— іноді NPC поводяться криво
— частина анімацій все ще дерев’яна
— деякі механіки можуть задушити нових гравців у перші години.
Проте чесно — всі ці мінуси губляться на фоні того, наскільки це велика і душевна RPG. За 280 годин мені жодного разу не було шкода витраченого часу. Навпаки — хотілося ще.
Kingdom Come: Deliverance 2 — це рідкісний випадок, коли продовження не просто краще за оригінал, а реально розвиває всі його ідеї. Якщо вам подобалась перша частина — тут ви будете як вдома. Якщо не грали — це одна з найкращих RPG останніх років. Ця гра повністю коштує свого часу та грошей.

TLDR, the game feels too clunky and unsatisfying to ask players to invest 15+ hours in the tutorial section before it "gets good". If you don't like the combat, this game isn't for you.
----------

For context, I'm a huge fan of immersive sims, which is why I bought the game. I love deus ex, dishonored, and survival modes in fallout and skyrim. KCD2 did not scratch that itch, for me.

I heard this game gets good after the tutorial area, but I'm 15+ hours in and still slogging away. The quests and dialogue seem to drag on too long. IMO, a lot of the characters are written to be rude or unlikeable, so it was hard for me to get invested in the story. I decided to explore the world, and although there was a lot to discover, none of it felt particularly interesting or useful. It felt like the game wanted me to do the main quest. 

Personally, I found the combat to be unplayable. I imagine it gets better later on, but your character is so weak in the beginning, and the fighting is so wonky at the best of times. I spent hours practicing and really tried to get invested. I bought perks, I trained with NPCs, stocked up on good weapons and armor, but none of that can fix jank. Combined with a resource-based save system, you can lose a lot of progress while you're learning the ropes. 

Aside from combat, I expected to spend a lot of time getting invested in the crafting (smithing, alchemy, etc). I had heard they were slow, which I don't mind, but I didn't expect them to be tedious. The crafting doesn't seem to require, encourage, or even allow for critical thinking, so there wasn't enough depth to sustain those mechanics by themselves.

At the 15 hour mark, I decided to stop and ask myself what the game seemed to be about and whether that core mechanic was any good. For a "realistic medieval sim", it felt like the game was mostly about combat, which was disappointing. And if you don't like the wonky combat, this game probably isn't for you.

Game has a lot to offer, but starting out the save system is just punishing. I really enjoyed getting lost in the woods exploring, gathering, hunting, fighting... but maybe on that 4th bandit encounter they get that lucky swing in and you die. I don't begrudge that, I could do better, but why then rob me of the hours of progress prior to that?

Its not like sleeping or drinking that special potion have a real thematic reason for saving your game - you can even just exit the game to create a save point. So just do us all a favor and add an auto-save, don't make us trek back to a bed, alchemy table or break immersion by exiting the game to protect our progress. A periodic auto-save lets me keep enjoying the game.

The game runs flawlessly on Linux, with no signs of stuttering or crashes. I played with HDR enabled at 4K/120Hz, and the world of KCDII is simply stunning. The game even hovers around 30-40 FPS on Steam Deck. This is peak PC gaming experience. Nearly everything has been improved from the first game. Main menu backgrounds are immaculate. Objectively the best RPG of 2025, and subjectively my GOTY choice for the year. All I can say is many yanks were pizzled during my playthrough.

Almost 30 hours in and I'm really enjoying it so far. 

Being a huge fan of KCD 1 this game feels like an amazing upgrade to an already amazing game. I'm still in the Trosky region nad still have ♥♥♥♥ tonne of quests remaining. I'm holding of the wedding till I do all the quests in this region. 

The graphics are beautiful and the music, oh go the music. The first game had some of the best soundtracks in video games and I still hum them from time to time. This also seems to have amazing music.

Will update the review further, but for now it is a must buy for the KCD1 fans.

Expansive and beautiful game. Combat feels awesome and has a reasonably high skill ceiling. Swords become overpowered pretty quickly but moving to other weapons kept things fun. Some of the side quest tasks can feel very repetitive and forgettable, but overall I enjoyed a majority of the tasks and interactions. If anything, a lot of the boring quests still provide a reason to explore the beautiful world of KCD2 more thoroughly, and that to me is not so bad. 250 hours in, I'm still discovering new things in the game.

Ran very well with an RTX 3060 and a Ryzen 7 3700x on medium settings. Some infrequent lighting and graphical glitches but not enough to really bother me.

Completed the game with 0 meat eaten and 0 animals killed for Lent achievement, still went to hell for the immense amount of adultery committed. 10/10

Steam's review system isn't nuanced enough, so I want to say that this is still generally a worthwile game, but you need to know what you're getting into.

The ambiance is perfect. Beautifully rendered and grounded environments. Most of my time was staring into the horizon and little nooks in the scenery indoors. It's so well lit, that my phone took better screenshots than whatever HDR-to-SDR conversion happened in the computer. The fricking 3D paperdoll rotating inventory is so cool I would sometimes just switch tabs idly to see it go.

The story is enjoyable, a good context and motivation for the player's action. But is in equal measures varied and incredibly railroaded.

Some (main quest) missions have only 1 correct solution, and if your build is not tailored to that you are just ♥♥♥♥♥♥, go load a previous save and grind ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ stealth or alchemy or whatever it is. The world is very detailed visually, but it's all very "look, don't touch". You think there would be for example a way to bypass stealth with craftsmanship in a certain place, but nope, you have to take the 1 exact route the designer chose.

There's also some clear "sequel one-upsmanship" going on here, which usually play into the railroading. There could be a straightforward solution in the moment, but the game does not give you the tools to take it. I can't elaborate without getting into spoilers, but it really feels sometimes like watching a horror movie protagonist do something clearly stupid even in context.

It's a movie with a lot of gameplay, to the point that it might actually have been better served by being a sandbox open world game with an accopmpanying movie, I feel like it would have been less frustratng that way.

I was shown this game by, a twitch streamer and holy cannoli is it good. Yeah the combat took me a min to get, but overall I love everything about this game. By my honor Henry, we shall be victorious.
You can't save the game when you want, also its a milsim game, there is no Easy or normal difficulty, 1 bandit, or just 1 wild dog can kill you easily. Hard

I get why they have the reduced save function- for immersion and making decisions feel permanent. However, this only works if your game is a razor-tight experience.

I've lost the vast majority of the hours I've played in this game due to totally random events that result in my death where I don't even know what is going on, which is oddly punishing early on when any guy in armour will absolutely rock your ♥♥♥♥.

Considering I'm doing a "good" run because I don't like stealing or aggroing and stuff keeps happening to me- bit strange.

This game is far too punishing for me to recommend to anyone with a job and finite gaming time.
Can't believe i never reviewed this, amazing game! i loved the first one many years ago, this is just massive improvements across the board, outstanding game!

KCD2 is the best game I've ever played. It's so fleshed out in terms of what you can do, the attention to detail is almost impossible to believe. Not only is it by far the most beautiful game I've played but the gameplay is of an equal level, from the invigorating combat to the interesting dialog options, this game offers a satisfying level of flexibility in your playstyle allowing you to approach every aspect in any way thought possible.

I've truly never experienced a game that immersed me as much as this one, the world genuinely feels alive and you really are Henry of Skalitz, I was blown away when I walked into Kuttenberg for the first time, the bustle of people, the noises, the architecture, simply incredible. And that level of immersion is only possible due to the brilliant writing, acting, and world design, not to mention how historically authentic this game is.

You'd be doing yourself a disservice if you don't decide to pick this game up and give it a go. If you don't think it will be for you, wait for a sale, I saw it for around £25 a while ago, that's an absolute steal and I can guarantee that you will find the game to be worth the money. Buy the game and get to the task!

Thank you Warhorse for this masterpiece, I look forward to the future experiences you plan to give us!

Even better than the first game — and the first was already great.
One of the best games I've ever played. A lot of interesting characters, especially Dry Devil and Zizka. The first 5–10 hours are a struggle, but after that the game feels much easier than the first part. [spoiler]I especially recommend getting Brunswick's Armor from the Lion's Crest quest — it's very helpful toward the end of the first act.[/spoiler] The Forge DLC is amazing: you can earn a lot of groschen while chilling and forging (swords, axes, etc.).
I'd also like to say a few words about the quests. They're fantastic and have a ton of variety — you can complete them in very different ways (by stealth, by force, by charisma, etc.).
Bottom line: if you love story-driven RPGs, medieval history, or realistic combat, this is an easy recommendation. Push through the slow opening and you'll find one of the most rewarding games out there.

This is a blast to play. The story and characters are incredibly engaging, and I never once got bored playing through the main story and side quests. While the combat takes a moment to learn, it is incredibly fun once you get the hang of it. I also "borrowed" way more loot from shops than I care to admit—and I don't regret a single thing. You should absolutely pick this game up and give it a try.

10/10 – I started this game a free man. I am now emotionally indebted to a fictional blacksmith's son from 15th century Bohemia.
Tom McKay put in 508 hours of dialogue recording for this thing. FIVE HUNDRED AND EIGHT HOURS. I have not worked 508 hours at anything in my entire life including sleeping. The man basically lived as a medieval peasant longer than some people's pregnancies, and it shows. Henry feels less like a video game protagonist and more like a guy who would genuinely lecture you about sword maintenance at a party and somehow you'd stay for the whole thing.
I also put in 508 hours. Tom McKay spent his recording dialogue. I spent mine on the lockpicking minigame. The lockpicking minigame was designed by someone who genuinely hates you. I have performed surgery with steadier hands. We both suffered. Only one of us got paid.
The combat will humble you. Not "oops I died" humble. "Sit in a chair and think about your life choices" humble. I got beaten up by a farmer. Twice. He had a stick.
The world is so deep and alive that I forgot I had a job for three days. My dog was fed. Barely. The laundry did not get done.
If you like history, swords, getting your feelings hurt by peasants, or just watching Tom McKay carry an entire game on his back like a medieval packhorse, buy this game immediately.
Your real life will still be there when you're done. Probably.

Felt quite hungry as Henry. Very awesome game everyone should experience atleast once. Cutscenes are beautiful and the dialogue is amazing. Henry is a very likeable character that's up there with Arthur Morgan and Geralt of Rivia for me. PLEASE play this game :)
So many wonderful things so say. The writing, the gameplay, the pacing. This is a true summit of achievment, videogame-wise.

I’ve played through both Part 1 and Part 2 at least twice, but I still can’t see what’s so good about the game.
Now I’m waiting for Part 3 to finally find out where the game is actually good. 
_(:3 / Z)_

This is a wonderfully immersive world that feels very real. The NPCs feel like they actually exist in the world and move through it. Having said that, the combat is very unforgiving, and you will die so many times early on especially. It takes some patience and fortitude to actually get into this game, which is really a life-in-1409 simulator.

Man... it's frickin beautiful. Easy to learn and frickin immaculate. I'm at a lost for words. The combat in the early game is extremely frustrating and may be hard to get a firm grip on for your first time playing. But I will tell you this, trainers!!! In the early game, head to Semine to train with captain Gnarly so that you can improve your stamina.   Please get this game, it will fill the empty holes in your heart.

Story is great but combat and horse mechanics are ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ terrible. Significant downgrades from KC:D 1.
Examples:
1. When fully armored, swords still damage through plate armor, when they do 0 IRL. In previous game, they would do 0 or 1 damage to full plate. They would bloody your screen, as they still do (stupid), but wouldn't damage you. Downgrade.
2. Blocking enemy attack while failing perfect block still makes you take damage, when it shouldn't and instead should just not allow for immediate riposte, to incentivize perfect blocks. If you hold block and get attacked, you'll see the weapon get blocked and not hit you, yet you still take massive damage, even wearing complete full plate protection. Downgrade.
- If 1. and 2. were fixed, combat would be a significant upgrade because there's no more master-strike hunting.
3. Horse winnies when you touch anything. ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ annoying.
4. Horse is reset to default and has no courage. You will get thrown off horse every time you encounter enemies at all, even if you get 50+ft away. Should get to choose starting horse to continue from KC:D 1. 
5. Horse throws you more often when head collides with barn and similar doorways. KC:D 1 was more forgiving and more fun. Being thrown from horse is a boring mechanic. Team fixed some boring mechanics, like bushes being 100% solid and sometimes untraversable, yet not this one. They worsened this one.
6. Horse now throws you if you sprint/spur too much in a row, unlike trained horse IRL and previous game where horse just runs out of stamina. Annoying. 
7. Horse handling is worse than previous game. More latency.
8. Must manually re-spur horse to continue sprinting, instead of just holding it. Probably makes sense because horse IRL needs to be re-told to continue sprinting, and wouldn't know via magic. Less fun though.

Everything else in game is as good or better than previous. Quests. Stories. Humor. Content. Etc.

But because combat is significantly worse, fighting multiple enemies is more punishing, and it's harder to make money with combat, so game is more grindy. Beat previous game without save-scumming, on hardcore and all perks. Combat is much worse and less realistic in this game. 

Still 9/10 game. Don't play it for the combat. If combat was skippable, I'd choose that, even though I play medieval games for the swordplay. 

More proof that doubling your dev team does not double quality in all aspects. Some key features can still be ♥♥♥♥♥♥ up or ignored.

Very immersive medieval sim, it's best to take it slow and enjoy the experience.
Hardcore is great if you have itch for even more realism.

KCD2 isn’t a game for people who skip dialogue. It’s for players who want to truly live in the medieval world — dirty, immersive, hilarious, and completely addictive.

10/10
“Just one more quest” at 3 AM simulator.

I just finished major part of the main campaign and most of the content. I have to say this is one of the best gaming experiences i have ever had in gaming in recent years. If you are a sucker for medieval history, Brutal FPS combat and war stories, this is seriously a no brainer.

Once your gang is reunited, the game delivers epic moments back to back with the best background score i have ever experienced.

It's Judgement day and i have goosebumbps all over- i want to now play it all over again in Hardcore mode. I am gonna miss playing it for the first time. 10/10.

Having just finished it, I can now say this is one of my favorite action RPG's. The gameplay, characters, world design, and story are all top notch. It had me laughing, gripping the edge of my seat, and feeling included. Though it is difficult to start off, once you get used to the systems and the combat, it gets much easier and rewarding. Already looking forward to eventually playing it again, and I hope the studio continues this kind of work.

after 72hrs of playing this game, I now have the burning desire to build a farmstead, pray to thy lord and marry off to a loving lady of noble status. 
This game has been a 100/100 game through and through. all aspects are just about perfect.

Amazing game. Great story, great gameplay, great OST and a great world to play in. 

Only issue I have is that the combat is slightly easier compared to the first game and there doesn't seem to be much of a reason to choose different type of melee weapons from my personal experience. 

Currently slowly working my way through a hardcore save and I'm still feeling the same joy I had the first time I played this game trying to discover the different routes one can take through the story and any side quests I missed.
One of the best games i have played a year ago i voted for exp 33 bcoz i didnt play this game and i have made a mistake

I’ve been playing KCD2 for around 8 hours now, and I feel like I can already tell who this game is really made for.

For Players Who Love Immersion and Realism
The game genuinely makes you feel like you’re living in 15th-century Europe. It’s not fantasy like Skyrim. No dragons, no magic, just swords, mud, hunger, exhaustion, and everyday life. Everything feels grounded and believable.

For People Who Enjoy Slow Growth and Learning
At the start, your character is honestly bad at almost everything, and even combat can feel awkward and frustrating. You don’t begin as some legendary warrior. You’re just a regular guy slowly learning how to fight, ride, read, and survive. If you enjoy the feeling of improving over time and earning your progress, this game does that really well.

For History and Detail Fans
The towns, forests, clothing, dialogue, and atmosphere all feel very authentic. Sometimes I find the game relaxing even when I’m not doing anything important, just walking through the woods or riding between villages while listening to the ambient sounds.

For Patient Gamers
This probably won’t be everyone’s type of game. If you want nonstop action, constant rewards, or fast pacing, you might get bored. The game takes its time and expects patience from the player, especially with traveling, combat, and resource management. As someone who loved Red Dead Redemption 2, that slower pace is actually one of the reasons I enjoy it.

For Fans of Realistic Combat
The combat feels weird at first, maybe even clunky, but once it starts to click, it becomes really satisfying. Fights feel more like actual duels than random button mashing.

Who Might Not Enjoy It?
Players who prefer fast progression, cinematic storytelling every few minutes, or simpler combat systems may not connect with it. The game also has a lot of dialogue, reading, and slower moments, which won’t appeal to everyone.

One Thing I Really Like About the Game
Honestly, KCD2 feels less like a game and more like a second life sometimes. Waking up at an inn, fixing your gear, eating, riding through forests quietly… the immersion is kind of crazy. As a person who works in an office setting during the day...there’s something really relaxing about coming home and slowing down in such a grounded world.

One thing I unexpectedly appreciated about the game is how humble it feels. A lot of games make you feel powerful from the very beginning, but KCD2 constantly reminds you that you’re just a human being learning through mistakes, struggle, and experience. It actually reminded me of two verses in the Qur’an, the holy book of Muslims: “And do not walk upon the earth arrogantly,” and another verse that says, “Indeed, mankind was created weak.” It was interesting how naturally the game reflects those ideas.
I played the first game, and I loved it. I finished this one, and it was even better. An adventure through and through, and I'll never forget it
A better “roleplaying” game than BG3, which is already an awesome game in its own right.
fun, realistic, and supports a lot of different playstyles
first game ive played that being an alcoholic is a useful skill, fantastic 10/10 experience would drink 13 schnapps in a row again

KCD2 is one of the most immersive and genuinely fun RPGs I’ve ever played. The first thing I have to mention is how polished and refined everything feels compared to KCD 1. The original had incredible ambition but could be rough technically at times, like awkward animations, bugs, and clunky game systems (combat especially). This sequel feels like the version the developers always wanted to make. Performance is massively improved, animations are smoother, combat is smoother and feels intentional, game systems are more fleshed out and very rewarding, NPC behavior feels life like, and the overall art style is on another level.

The world itself is honestly the star of the game. Unlike most aesthetically medieval games that attempt to romanticizes the medieval period, It's grounded in brutal realism. A realism that showcases the systemic hardships of the average person in medieval Bohemia, especially the class dynamics between nobility, clergy, and peasants. A snapshot of a time of turmoil in the countryside of medieval Bohemia. Forests feel dense, alive, and untamed. Traveling feels precarious, never knowing where bandits may pop out of, the roads tell a story of travelers past, leaving traces behind. Towns and the city are packed with activity, each one has their own identity, their own flavor. But with it's brutal realism also comes the honest and simple whimsy of everyday life. You’re not just moving between quest markers, you genuinely feel like you’re traveling through a lived-in world that does not consider you the main character. You are only a person that exists within it and will continue to exist when you leave. Besides that, the weather systems, lighting, environmental sound design, and environmental detail all come together to create the most cinematic experience I have ever had.

Another favorite system I enjoy a lot is the social mechanics in the world of KCD 2. The world is reactive to every action that you take in-game, from the clothing you wear, to the demeaner you choose to express to others, status effects, being covered in mud/blood, relationship to relevant NPCs, relationship to each socio-economic class, how you're perceived in each township/area from the actions you've made in the area. All these instances contribute to how the world receives you.

10/10 This game is one of my favs of all time.

I played the first game and didn't like it much. Good graphics and an authentic medieval atmosphere didn't make up for the clunky combat, buggy/convoluted quests and janky NPCs. However, I decided to give the second game a try as it was on sale, and almost all the reviews were glowingly positive.

And guess what? The sequel is more of the same. In fact, I would say that the quests were even buggier than the last game, with quest items suddenly disappearing, NPCs turning hostile for no good reason even though I'd just spent hours jumping through hoops to please them, and combat mechanics so crude it was practically impossible to win any fight (even against a single wild dog) unless through sheer luck.

It mystifies me as to how this game got such good reviews and garnered awards the way it did. For me, playing was a deeply frustrating and annoying experience I wouldn't care to repeat.
10/10. This game feels made with so much care that even the side quests and tasks are more fun than the main quests in most games I’ve played.
It's a beautiful game with amazing visuals and voice acting unfortunately the in game mechanics especially combat are dreadful, it feels incredibly clunky and isn't fun.

This is probably my favorite game series.  Story is great, characters are great, I like the challenge at the start but how it gets to where you're able to build your skills (and Henry's) until the game gets a bit easier.  The gameplay loop is great.  I usually prefer when games let you your own character but I didn't even mind not being able to in KCD1&2 because Henry is great.

I was a little sad they removed the quick-brew option from the first game, which felt like a reward after gaining enough brewing experience.  The horseriding I liked marginally better in KCD1 as well (KCD2 tipped the scales making it a little more realistic but also less convenient than KCD1 in my eyes), but besides these two things everything else was an improvement in KCD2 in my opinion.  The combat, the graphics, the world and cities/towns, and performance seems a lot smoother this time around.  

I think they could afford to give an 'easy mode' option to free-save whenever for those that think needing a potion to save is silly.  I don't mind it because I like the potion brewing process, but from my point of view this mechanic could possibly be one of the biggest hurdles to getting new players to try this game.  I've played through both games twice and for both games my second playthrough I downloaded a mod to allow free-saving, and it feels a bit unfair that it isn't an option for console players.
Genuinely the best game I have ever played. Done several playthroughs thus far, and intend to do more. Cannot wait for the sequel.

It's a great game, it's not perfect. What they do well is done very well, what they get wrong they get REALLY wrong, particularly:

-combat (obviously). every bandit in the game seems to be able to one shot you with crude weapons, whereas you have to land multiple blows to kill someone wearing no armor. it's not fun, neither are the combat mechanics, and the game cheats. luckily i found that bane poison on my crossbow bolts would usually one shot most people. it feels cheap but it's only fair since you'll spend multiple hours redoing things because of combat (on normal difficulty).

-i got sick of my dog constantly barking at everything, i don't even play with him anymore. any advantage to him is not worth it when he reminds me of the neighborhood dogs I can't get away from in real life. plus he loves to sit in doorways and block my path, or run underneath my feet and slow me up. 

-as with most open world games like this, there are countless bugs that break the suspension, which is a shame because the game is really good at sucking you into the world and making you feel emersed in a random moment. I got hard locked out of finishing a conversation to complete a quest because I failed the skill check within the conversation. luckily i was still able to complete the quest, but i'd rather just be able to attempt the skill check again instead of having to look up online what the next step is.

-the acting and plot were often done so badly. i ended up hating catherine because her line delivery was just so over the top in certain scenes, and often the quests just turned into a wild goose chase. I needed to find this magical stone, it got stolen from one person, and the next person sold it to someone in another town, to someone who got captured in another town, and so on. you get the stone eventually and they give you 50 coin for an hour of gameplay that was so unrewarding. often times the game feels like it just wants to eat up your time with dumb quests like this that had the same formula, and it too often relied on a combat system that I didn't want to involve myself in.

I haven't gotten this annoyed at a game in a while, but it's impossible to not give this a positive rating, i love this game (and the first one), they're both must play games even with the glaring issues, this one is better than the last, the dlc so far has been great (only played the forge dlc but it's packed with missions with good rewards for progress), and I'm admittedly maybe halfway through the main story. the biggest miss so far is I wish i could craft my own armor, that seems like an obvious thing they'd add to this one, maybe next time, if there is a next one.
Honestly, just as good as Expedition33. Music, Story, Characters and Gameplay are some of the best ive ever seen. You are missing out not playing this game.

Loved the game, it's hilarious and well done. The combat is great.

The only critic I have is lack of replayability. There's not different "builds", you're kind of forced to use swords as you can only master strike with those and there's not much different gear (you can't only use bow, you kind of have 1 shot then have to melee). As opposed to similar games like Elder scrolls or Witcher games.

And this is NOT Game of the Year? Have you all lost your minds over there??? What even is Expedition 33 compared to this?
This is a game on the level of GTA and The Witcher. The story, gameplay, mechanics, atmosphere, immersion—everything is top-tier. Huge respect to everyone who worked on this game for what they've accomplished.

I went in with very high expectations, and the game still managed to exceed them. The biggest problem is that the side activities are too good—they keep distracting you from the main story, which is also amazing.
What I got from this game was a true journey and adventure. Highly recommended to everyone.

I wanted to like it. The combat is arguably the worst combat I have ever seen. 1v1 is fine, fun even. But the second you have a 2v1 it just becomes impossible... Maybe I suck, that's possible. But I have full cleared every dark souls game just fine and I was never as frustrated as I was with this game. I am sure the actual game is great and the story seemed good. But I can't get past how awful and clunky the combat felt to me.

Kingdom Come: Deliverance 2 is a deeply immersive medieval RPG that improves almost everything from the first game. Its realistic world, strong storytelling, and satisfying combat make every quest feel meaningful, while the detailed open world brings 15th-century Bohemia to life. Critics especially praised the freedom, roleplaying depth, and improved gameplay systems

I have now played both KCD and KCDII for over a month and a half and now that I have finished both I dont know what to do with my life, its like watching the best show you have ever seen on the regular and now its finished.  This is one, if not the greatest games I have ever played and urge you to buy it.

This game just entered my Mount Rushmore of narrative-heavy, open-world games.
 
Gameplay/Mechanics - One of the most in-depth RPG games out there. The sword fighting and horse back-riding are also very detailed and realistic. Very immersive. 10/10
Story/Narrative - Might have surpassed RDR2's in my opinion. 10/10
Characters - All of the main characters from the heroes down to the villains, are memorable. 10/10
Quests/Side quests - For a game this massive, this is probably one of the rare ones that quests, whether main quests or side quests, didn't feel repetitive at all. 10/10
The Open world - Not only one of the most beautiful, but also one of the most felt-alive open world games out there. Thanks to the programming of the NPCs lol. 10/10 
The Artwork - Gotta give props to the artists that made the artwork on this game, because those are beautiful! 10/10 
Replayability - This is probably the only downside of this game as this game will consume you so much that you probably wouldn't want to play it all over again after you've beaten it. 6 /10
Amazing game. Story was top notch, combat was hard at first, but was rewarding once you get your stride. Haven't been this impressed with a game in a while!
Good Game , 10/10 would go back to bathhouse to get my pizzle nizzled
Amazing game. Many improvements made between KCD1 and KCD2 but they were able to keep what made KCD1 great.
Scratches the same itch as Skyrim. Very fun and well-thought-out game

This game is infinitely better than the first Kingdom Come: Deliverance.
I'd even go so far as to say buying the first game before this one to play chronologically is a terrible idea.
It covers the major points of the first game quite well without giving too long of a recap.

The combat is vastly improved, fluid, and has multiple execution points so its not stagnant like the first game.
The combo system from the first game is vastly improved and this time actually useable.
Multi-enemy combat is vastly improved, and not as much of a pain, you can pivot around the enemies to block them from cleaving your back, and they rarely dash around behind you and gain a massive advantage. Its almost infinitely better I'd say.
You can also block enemies that you aren't locked on which is a vast improvement over the first game.
And while there are still animations that fling your camera around, they aren't anywhere near as bad or common as the first game, and the combat animations in general feel nice and make sense.
The clinch system is an absolute amazing rework, the skill checks in both that and the knockout and stealth kill system are just amazing.
Masterstrikes are an amazing addition as well.

Archery is vastly improved and aiming isn't as much of a pain as the first game, you can actually use a ranged weapon at the start of the game and hit the enemy.

The skills system is great, not perfect, but great.
Some skills and their perks are weird, take crafting for example, why is a stolen items perk in the crafting skill.
Along with that the blacksmithing, there should be more perks and general effects relating specifically to the blacksmithing.
However I cant complain too much, just some frustration I had while using the system early on.
At least to me it wasn't well explained, though I eventually learned the inner workings through trial and error, which was nice.

Even after nearly twice the amount of hours as the first game I have been drawn to the environment and find myself simply riding my horse through the roads or frolicking through the forests and fields.
The visuals are simply amazing, a vast improvement over the first game, which while had good visuals, weren't any thing above standard. Albeit the setting of Eastern Europe was very fantastical.
The world feels so alive even in the most quiet of forests, and the roads are teeming with caravans, wayfarers, and bandits.
The event system is quite fluid, finding a bandit on the road, unlike the last game, is interesting, you can interact with them first, taunt them, or question them.
Which if directly compared to the first game, its black and white, in the first you'd either have a band of peasants with clubs and dogs bash your skull in or some friendly event.
The camps along the road are also a nice way to disperse events.

In general I consider this game an infinite improvement over the first, well worth buying even at full price.


Extra comments on the game, dice is an amazing minigame that you can genuinely spend lots of time on.

Thievery is a bit overpowered, at the higher level you can literally rob everyone and gain way too much way too early, at least in my opinion, there should have been more levels to locks and pickpocketing.
But I wont complain about that too much as its an advantage players can choose not to take.

The blacksmithing is an amazing addition, though why cant you forge polearms, most of them are single bladed weapons so they should be forgeable.

The variety of horses is amazing, I do wish there were more unique horses though, especially more foreign ones.
When I did that quest to get Ashbourne, it was almost an ethereal experience, mystical and mythical.
Speaking of, after discovering that horses owner, its name should be changed to its proper name.
And I wish the NPCs reacted to it uniquely, its genuinely on the level of a mythical beast.
For reference they just comment about the price of your horse.
More things like that would be absolutely amazing, even if not truthfully mythical.

Armor and clothing options are amazing, and fit well in comparison to the first game.
They can be mixed in a visually appealing way, and the way the NPCs wear a variety of clothing is nice.
I like that there are many metal finishes on the armors, and even that some of them have dents and bangs that are permanent, even when fully repaired.

Насколько красивая, настолько и кривая игра. Постоянные баги, вроде союзника Коряги, что переключается  с мечом на тебя, как только перебьешь всех врагов; или нпс, которые нормально общаются с тобой, пока ты у них за спиной, но как только они разворачиваются к тебе лицом, то тут же вспоминают, что ты в этой деревне преступник и тебя надо наебашить. Боевка здесь -- чуть меньшее говно, чем в первой части, но все равно не играбельна.

Мини-игры вроде алхимии и кузнечки хорошие первые пару раз, а потом превращаются в проверку на резиновость твоего терпения. Да и выхлоп с них небольшой, торговцы занижают цены на твои товары будь здоров.

Не знаю, что там по основному квесту, но практически все побочки сводятся к "Потерялся X, иди найди".   Скучно

Единственное, что понравилось, это фишка жесткого режима с отключением маркера игрока на карте. Ориентироваться на местности было интересно. В остальном, проходная игра

You start the game poorer than the lowest beggar with a dirty shirt and a shovel.  By the time you've finished the most expensive armor and weapons are yours to rule over every encounter and fight.  Stealing stealthily is your friend.  But remember that if anyone sees you before you steal you will be blamed.  So what you do is wait for night travel to a town during the night then steal and leave the town go to another town.  If anyone sees you during your thefts you'll be in a bit of hot water.  Good sneaking to you!

deserves, goty, the kcds series is simply the best medieval games series and not fantasy, just pure history and medieval experience. literally almost everything in the game is realistic, only misses pooping
I can’t describe how great this game is. It took me months to understand everything I must say. But, how gorgeous rpg. I’ve played in pc gamepass but I’m currently switching to the steam version.

Overall it's a solid and immersive game. Combat mechanics are clunky to get the hang of at first, and afterwards they are easy to cheese, but still pretty cool. Unfortunately, what stopped me from continuing was the constant quests where you just slowly follow someone on foot or your horse with no ability to skip should you want to. But if that doesn't bother you then the storyline and quests will be very interesting and engaging.

After playing Kingdom Come: Deliverance II for more than 60 hours, I can confidently say this game is amazing. The characters, side quests, main story set pieces, and the sheer amount of freedom given to the player are absolutely mind-blowing. Yes, I encountered a few glitches here and there, but they never took away from the overall experience.

"Audentes Fortuna Iuvat" ("Fortune favors the brave") perfectly sums up the journey. From Henry of Skalitz, Hans Capon, Katherine, Rosa, Godwin, Žižka, Dry Devil, and so many others, the cast is memorable and incredibly well-written. The story is excellent, the side quests are among the best I've played in an RPG, and the mix of serious drama, humor, and unexpected moments keeps the experience fresh throughout. In the end it actually made me cry!!!!

This game has everything I want from a role-playing game: immersion, meaningful choices, memorable characters, freedom, and rewarding exploration.

10/10.
one of the best games I've ever played in my life, easily able to come back after months of not playing it and still able to find things to do in it

I didn't expect to enjoy an RPG this much after only a few hours, nor did I expect to sink so many hours into it in such a short amount of time, hehe. Honestly, I'm speechless.

The level of detail and care from the developers is on another level, even compared to The Witcher. The quests range from pure comedy to absolute heartbreak. The game is incredibly well made and difficult in a way that it probably doesn't need to be, but that's exactly what makes it so good.

You'll die to a random beggar just as easily as you would to a knight. You'll get mocked and treated like dirt all the same. And if you try to sneak into someone's house and sleep there, be ready to get your ass kicked too.

I merely wanted to deliver a message to Von Bergov in Trosky.

191 hours later, that is the only thing I couldn't do. Wonderful game.

I loved this game and got my moneys worth for sure.  That said, it's really bogged down in the middle where you're forced to put together a little band of cutthroats to rescue your buddy.  I'm just so frekking bored during this section.  It is seeming to go on and on.  Can someone who's pushed past this middle drag give me some hope to hang in there.  Does it get back to an awesome fun story.  I feel like after I busted Franz out the second or third time it just started to drag too much.  No spoilers... just some reasons to hang in.
I liked the game, Things that annoy me?  walking a hundred miles overburdened because Im in the beginning of the game and I want to sell the loot for 2 coppers.

I've finally beat the game at 180 hours in. It might be the one RPG I've played that truly sucked me into the world. Might even Dethrone Fallout New Vegas for my favorite RPG. The first main quest handed to me was to attend a wedding, it took me 60 hours to attend said wedding because of all the side missions kept pulling me away. Almost every side mission was interesting and kept me engaged. 

The Cutscenes are movie like and carry suspense at times, but also a lot of comedic moments. Its like "a Knights Tale" movie at times. If you seen the movie, it pretty much sums up the experience you'll have playing the game.

The world itself is very convincing and draws me in to explore it. the two maps are based off of real locations and the castles are also based off of the real ones. the world is not like alot of RPGs that have a giant map, but nothing but empty terrain in-between missions. its filled with interesting places. if the area is bare, it usually houses an easteregg or hidden treasure stash. when it starts to rain in game, it feels like it really raining. the atmosphere and sound design of the storms makes it different than other games.

The NPCs are more life like in this game compared to other RPGs. your not gonna just but a bucket on a head and steal all the cheese because the crime system is good. If the shop keeper saw you walk in, he finds out that a particular item is missing. He will look for it, tell the guards about it. If you are using said item around him, he will respond in kindly about it. 

The game will start off slow and punish you for trying to rush it. your not a superhero yet, so you will lose the first few fights. you have to really learn the combat style of the game. you have to adopt to its rules or its gonna chew you up. The combat mechanics is a direct improvement over the first game, but still has its flaws in npc response time and locking on target/switching targets can be awkward at times. battles between more than 2v2 type turns into a drunken brawl with swords, making it pretty cheesy battling. To sum up combat, take you time learning it and practice fighting until you got it down. the dog makes combat easier, but i always sent it home.

Potion Brewing is a lot of fun, you take your time playing a mini game of brewing whatever potion you want to make. you have recipe book that has to be followed step by step. making the same potion a few times and you'll eventually make them without the book. any recipes you know before your character has learned yet can just be made. its a lot of fun. I spent a lot of time early game making them for money. Blacksmithing was also fun, but not nearly as good compared to potion brewing.

The game has bugs like a Bethesda game. but I never crashed throughout my playthrough. I've had two side quest break because a npc didn't spawn in correctly or at all. Characters not having cloths on during cutscenes. example of this was near the end of the game, Godwin was drinking and "lost" his cloths mid cutscene. standing behind the other lords on the wall while they talked an important convo with the enemy. I thought it was super funny.

I don't normally do reviews, which is why its a bit all over the place, but wanted my two cents to be throw in the wind. I'm looking forward to their Lord of the Rings game.
Great Game, very immersive and the fighting sequences are very challenging, not a walk ijn the park
Can't stop playing, love the story and open world

Probably one of the best games I've ever played. Incredible story and incredible mechanics. I love the combat, it becomes easier as the game goes along and is an improvement from KCD1. The story alone took me around 70 hours to complete, so I 100% believe I've gotten my moneys worth.

This game makes me feel like I had a past life in Bohemia. KCD2 really shows what life was like back then. I love KCD1 and I'm not surprised by this one either. Perfection. 

Only complaints which they have done A LOT better in this game is the cutscenes. Final scene at the end didn't show my henry's beard or armor that I had set for him. The game does tend to give you a whole set of armor for those to actually simulate what they weren't able to tackle during development. So you get a whole new set of armor for certain cutscenes lol. 

Combat big improvements all around although - they just need to commit and make polearms OP. I didn't see a big benefit from them. My longsword slaughtered all my foes with ease. Master strikes man. 

Fist fighting - they basically allow you to be a Bohemian Bruce Lee. Realistic too. You don't punch someone in the head if they have a helmet on. You PALM them for best results - can't crack your knuckles now. 

That's it. 

I'm Commander Shepherd and this is my favorite game on the Citadel.
Phenomenal follow up to phenomenal game, if you're looking for your next big RPG this is the one to get, dlcs and all!
Great storytelling. Very immersive and an amazing world to just be in and discover. Would recommend 10/10.

This will be the best RPG for a long, LONG time to come.

KCD 1 was already miles upon miles ahead of the market, and this game has perfected it, nothing comes close right now.

This game has absolutely mogged all current AAA studios, it shows what is possible if these studios just put just a pinch of effort into making a good game.

Easy 10/10

GTA/RDR2 player here probably first steam review. It took me a while to learn the KCD2 basics but this game is unbelievably worth every penny if you like a challenge and a stunning open simulated world this one being medieval
Wonderful game! Henry plowed all the wenches of Trosky and Kuttenberg region

Almost 400hrs from one playthrough

Take everything from the first game and make it better. These developers are passionate. They create games with worlds that feel lived in, NPCs that feel smart and lively, set in a wildly accurate historical era and loosely based on real events. The history aspect especially makes the early Assassins Creed lover in me smile.

That said, the game is grounded and slow, the combat is tricky at first, and at the beginning it feels like Henry kinda sucks at everything. But the level progression compounds, and by the end you truly feel like an unstoppable knight.
This is one of the best RPGs you can play. The first 10 hours are brutal, but the payoff is worth it.

What can I say... it wins the awards it does for a reason. It's one of those games that defines a chapter of your life and creates instant nostalgia. A year after setting it down, you'll consider playing again and find yourself preparing to be ported to the time you first played it (and of course 15th century bohemia). This is what games used to be and that we need about 100 more of, but the industry just doesn't allow it. Anyway, I'm ready to play it again and be ported to a time when I had more time and friends and capacity. I hope it's the same for you!

One of those RPGs that only come along every 5-10 years. Like Witcher 3, you will put 80-120h into it, love it and come back every few years to replay it. Also, as a fan of history, I think its a great depiction of medieval time brought to life.

[h1]The single greatest open world game ever made and will likely never be topped until KCD3.[/h1]
[list]
[*]Imagine Witcher 3 if it's combat was great.
[*]Imagine Skyrim but the main quest wasn't ass.
[*]Imagine almost matching Red Dead Redemption 2 quality but at 10% the budget.
[/list]

That is Kingdom Come Deliverance 2. It feels like you're playing through a multiple season show of HBO but you get to play and live inside this world with immersion that no other game would spend the time to let you do.  What once was a rough passion project is now somehow a mainstream appealing passion project about a real place and mostly real people that you will fall in love with just like I remember what happened with Witcher 2.

There is one downside, the game doesn't have churches you can enter like the first one. literally unplayable :(

I spent a few hours talking in-depth about the game here:
https://youtu.be/puAF2neiknI

can keeps crashing at splash image when you click on it and only work around is deleting shader cache and verifying the game files which is time consuming i dont want have to do that after like one or two game sessions

This is one of the best games I have ever played. It holds a sort of classic RPG vibe. The Story is fantastic and very immersive. The story is not a story that focuses on the world around you so much it is more about your story as the Henry. This means there are many ways for quests to be completed it gives your choices an impact on how well your story is going and will affect how you are perceived. The reputation and leveling systems in this game are great. The combat feels more realistic. This game will draw you in so you have been warned.

[h3] A great sequel to a great story [/h3]

I enjoyed the original game because of how it delivered on the story and the characters. The sequel takes on that and improves on aspects of scale and technical excellence.

The adventures of Henry of Skalitz continue in a big world. He meets many peculiar individuals and faces multiple challenges that can be played in various ways. Play style variety, role-playing, and decision-making are some of the strongest assets of the game.

The scale of the game is massive compared to the first part. It concerns both the physical world size and the activities it offers. From the technical perspective, KCD2 looks and runs great even on my Steam Deck. If you loved the first game as strongly as I did, you absolutely must play the sequel!

This game has easily earned a spot in my top 5 games of all time. I’m already 150+ hours in and somehow I still haven’t finished it, which says a lot about how much there is to do and how much I enjoy just existing in its world. The attention to detail, the atmosphere, the characters, and the feeling of genuinely living through Henry’s journey are incredible. Every time I tell myself I’ll focus on the main story, I end up getting lost in another adventure. It’s one of those rare games that makes you forget about the real world for a while, and I honestly love it so much.

Really love this game it has great fighting mechanics, the storyline is on par with RDR2. The horse, stealing, and haggling is amazing, the fact that you can buy something for 30 grochen and manage to haggle it down to 15 grochen (depending on the strength of your charisma and your popluarity in that twon. Overall gameplay is AMAZING and a MUST BUY!!!

Great game!
BUT not a great game to get into. I had to look up some basic things on youtube to understand the game better. Once I did that, it really became amazing.

I’m only about 10+ hours in so far, but I already would recommend this game to any RPG lover. 

The game mechanics id say certainly are more difficult and immersive than most games, which at times can be quite frustrating but equally very rewarding - and the only community and guides surrounding this game are fantastic and very welcoming. 

If you’re into CP2077 and RDR2 and similar story driven games like that, you’d be silly not to give it a try

After KCD1, the 2 really delivers the updated and upgraded version of the game.

The awesome atmosphere is there, you can really experience being in the scorching Bohemia, on meadows and forests.

Smithing, alchemy, fighting and all those things combined, along with the, sometimes, unrelenting main story, this game is a fine piece.
One of the best medivial games, the realism is on point and you can do literally anything.

The new standard I'll judge any other open world game by.
